\begin{abstract}
\section{Abstract}

Checking one agent is easy: wrap it in ``if the check fails, run it again.'' That trick
assumes the world holds still between the check and the act. \textbf{In a fleet of concurrent agents
it does not.} A check reads \emph{true} when agent~A looks and \emph{false} by the
time A writes, because agent~B changed the shared state in between (a
time-of-check-to-time-of-use race\cite{toctou}, lifted onto world state). Agents act through
high-level tool APIs that offer no transaction and no conditional write, so no re-run wrapper around A
can close that race. It takes a \textbf{referee between the agents} that serializes their
effects and reads only bytes the agents \emph{cannot author}. We build one and run it live. The
finding that surprised us is about \emph{where} a non-forgeable verdict is worth spending. Hand it
\emph{back} to the agent that earned it (\textbf{in-loop}) and it is flat-to-harmful: a
capable agent re-checks and heals its own phantom, so every active fix we measured (warn, rewind,
inject a cure) only disturbs a run that would have passed. The lone in-loop survivor is the
\emph{negative} action, \textbf{give up correctly} (zero false-halts across 1,634 winners,
$\sim$11\% compute saved). Hand the same verdict to \textbf{anyone else} --- the rest of the
fleet --- and it pays, on both of the ways a fleet fails. Off the environment's own
database hash (a byte no agent wrote), an arbiter serializes eight pairs of racing agents to prevent
\textbf{all 6 would-be lost-update clobbers}, and a write-admission gate blocks \textbf{10
genuine over-claims across 120 clean tasks} while admitting every correct write. The block rate is an identical
8.3\% on a mid and a strong model, so the signal does not fade at the stronger tier. A third consumer
is not an agent at all. Each blocked over-claim is a \emph{poison reward label} a self-judged RL
loop would bank as a win, and scoring the reward against the byte the model cannot author --- the
verifiable-reward (RLVR\cite{rlvr}) discipline --- purges every one (label purity
60\%~$\rightarrow$~100\%). One non-forgeable check, three consumers: a
\textbf{monitorability} primitive for fleets and the loops that train them, whose value turns
on \emph{where} it is spent.
\end{abstract}

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{hero_inloop_vs_outofloop}
  \caption{\cref{fig:hero}. Hold a check the agent \textbf{cannot forge} fixed and
ask only \emph{where} to spend it. \textbf{In-loop} (left), every active fix is
flat-to-harmful and only give-up (which writes nothing) survives; \textbf{out-of-loop}
(right), the same verdict pays on both fleet failures --- J~=~6 races serialized,
J~=~10 phantom writes refused. The asymmetry is the finding. (\textbf{J} is our one
score: an integer count of real corruptions the referee blocked off ground truth, never a rate; full
key in Appendix~A.)}
  \label{fig:hero}
\end{figure*}

\section{Introduction}\label{sec:intro}

Give an agent a real task --- reconcile an inventory across two SaaS apps, send a survey to
every customer --- and it works for many turns, then announces success. Often the announcement is
a lie the agent believes. It wrote ``All customers have now received their surveys!'' and
the surveys never went out. This is \emph{silent failure}: the run looks finished, the world is
still broken, and nothing in the agent's own words tells you which is true. For one agent the
skeptic reaches for a tidy answer, and at the easy hop it is right, so we grant it up front: do not
trust the ``done,'' check the work, re-run if the check fails. We grant that \emph{loop}
--- while noting two parts of it are real work the phrase ``just check the result''
hides: a check the agent cannot forge (not ``re-read your own result''), and sensing fast
enough to gate every turn (\cref{sec:positioning-wrapper}). But the claim we rest the paper on is
what a re-run wrapper genuinely \emph{cannot} reach. The instant you have a \emph{fleet} ---
many agents working concurrently against the same database, the same tickets --- the assumption
breaks: a check reads \emph{true} when agent~A looks and \emph{false} by the time A writes,
because agent~B changed the shared state in the gap (a time-of-check-to-time-of-use
race\cite{toctou}, lifted from files onto world state). The textbook cure --- fuse check and
write into one atomic step --- lives in the \emph{store}, and the high-level tool APIs agents act
through (cancel a reservation, file a ticket) expose only unconditional writes. So no re-run wrapper
around A can close the race. It takes a referee \emph{between} the agents that supplies the missing
serialization itself and trusts none of their narration. That referee, and the one surprising place
its verdict is worth spending, is this paper.

The trust rule under everything is one test: read what the agent \emph{says}, or read bytes the
agent \emph{could not have written}. We call the second \textbf{byte-clean} --- a check
whose byte-author is not the agent under judgement (an environment's tool result qualifies; the
agent's own prose does not). Reading the agent's own bytes is the \textbf{mirror-verifier
trap}: it measures whether the story is internally consistent, not whether it matches the
world, and collapses the moment the model stops emitting the give-away phrase you trained on. (A
plain-language walk-through of every term is in \textbf{Appendix A}; the machinery in
Appendix B.)

The result is not a detector but a place to \emph{spend} one's verdict. Hold a check the
agent cannot forge fixed, and ask only \emph{where}. Hand it \textbf{in-loop}, back to the
agent that earned it, and it is worthless: every active fix we measured was flat or worse, because
injecting any turn perturbs runs that would have passed. Hand it \textbf{out-of-loop}, to the
rest of the fleet, and it pays on the two distinct ways a fleet fails --- a \textbf{claim}
(one agent announces a write that did not land, and a peer inherits it as truth) and a
\textbf{race} (two agents touch the same state and one overwrites the other). In
\cref{sec:payoff} we build one out-of-loop referee reading the environment's own database
hash, run it live on tau2-bench\cite{tau2bench} through Gemini, and it blocks \textbf{10 real
over-claims across two models} at an identical 8.3\% rate while \textbf{serializing two live
agents across eight conflict pairs to prevent all six would-be clobbers} --- both off
ground truth, neither touching the judged agent. A third consumer is the loop that \emph{trains} the
next model: each blocked over-claim is a poison reward label a self-judged RL loop would bank as a
win, and scoring the reward against the same byte (RLVR\cite{rlvr} in the strict sense) purges
every one. The rest of the paper is the boundary that \emph{forces} this design.
One distinction throughout: \textbf{J counts real corruptions a non-forgeable witness blocked},
never a downstream-outcome delta; where we measure the latter ($\Delta$B) it is a fragile floor, and we
say so in the same breath.

\begin{quote}
\textbf{Why this matters past this paper.} A verdict the agent
\emph{cannot forge or train away} is the load-bearing primitive under three problems a frontier lab
already cares about, each touched with a measured result. \textbf{Scalable oversight\cite{bowman}}:
when work outruns a human's line-by-line check\cite{metr}, the only oversight that survives is
rooted in evidence the model did not author (the byte-clean rule and its rungs W0--W3,
\cref{sec:boundary}). \textbf{Reward hacking / monitorability\cite{baker}}: a self-judged
loop rewards the \emph{appearance} of success, and a label non-forgeable by narration is the one
signal optimization pressure cannot launder --- we show it purges real poison reward labels off
ground truth. \textbf{Multi-agent reliability}: the race no single-agent wrapper can reach
needs a referee \emph{between} agents, and we build one and measure the corruptions it prevents live.
The method borrows a minimal-trusted-kernel framing\cite{anderson}, three distributed-systems hazards
(TOCTOU\cite{toctou}, lost-update\cite{bernstein}, write-ahead recovery\cite{aries}), and the
SPRT\cite{wald}/CUSUM\cite{page} lineage of the give-up gate --- and it states the strength of
every claim in the same breath as the claim, never dressing a rate as a payoff or a frozen replay as a
live result.
\end{quote}
